eras.name: Maori Textile Traditions
eras.description: Maori weavers (primarily women of mana) developed extraordinary textile arts using native flax (harakeke). Their cloaks (kakahu) were among the most technically sophisticated textiles in the Pacific, with feather, dogskin, and taniko-bordered varieties denoting chiefly status.
keyGarments: Kaitaka (fine flax cloak with taniko border)
eras.aiImagePrompt: A Maori chief wearing a magnificent korowai cloak of brown kiwi feathers over a woven flax base, with taniko geometric borders, pounamu (greenstone) pendant, standing before a carved wharenui meeting house.

eras.name: Colonial & Pastoral Era
eras.description: British settlers brought Victorian fashion to New Zealand, adapted to a rugged pastoral landscape. The wool industry became the backbone of the economy, and practical farming attire evolved alongside formal colonial dress that arrived months behind London trends.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A late Victorian New Zealand sheep station scene with a woman in a practical wool riding habit and oilskin coat, Canterbury Plains with Southern Alps in the background, overcast light.

eras.name: Suburban & DIY Culture
eras.description: Post-war New Zealand fashion was characterized by British-influenced conservatism and a strong tradition of home sewing. Geographic isolation fostered self-reliance, with most women making their own garments from imported patterns and local wool.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A 1960s New Zealand suburban scene with a woman in a hand-sewn floral shift dress and hand-knitted cardigan, standing in a garden with a Hills Hoist clothesline, soft overcast southern light.

eras.name: NZ Avant-Garde Emergence
eras.description: New Zealand designers broke free from colonial conformity with radical, dark, and architecturally daring fashion. The Wellington and Dunedin scenes produced designers who channeled the wild landscape and isolation into internationally recognized avant-garde collections.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A moody 1990s New Zealand fashion editorial on a black sand beach, a model in a dramatic deconstructed black wool coat with raw edges, wild hair in the wind, Taranaki coastline, stormy sky.

eras.name: World Stage Breakthrough
eras.description: NZ designers achieved global recognition: Karen Walker's edgy femininity, Zambesi's dark minimalism, and World's conceptual collections. The Lord of the Rings films brought NZ craftsmanship to global attention, boosting the creative industries.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A New Zealand Fashion Week front row scene in Auckland, a model on the runway in a sculptural Karen Walker-style tailored coat in deep forest green, minimalist concrete venue, crisp editorial lighting.

eras.name: Sustainability & Maori Renaissance
eras.description: New Zealand has positioned itself at the forefront of sustainable fashion, leveraging its merino wool and natural fiber industries. Simultaneously, Maori design principles of kaitiakitanga (guardianship) are influencing a new wave of culturally grounded, environmentally conscious fashion.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A contemporary New Zealand fashion shoot in Fiordland, a model in a luxurious oversized merino wool coat with subtle taniko-inspired geometric trim, misty mountains and native bush in the background, soft natural light.

textiles.name: Maori Taniko Weaving
textiles.description: A geometric finger-weaving technique using dyed flax threads to create intricate border patterns on cloaks, with designs encoding tribal identity and spiritual meaning.
textiles.technique: Finger-weaving (twining) using prepared harakeke (New Zealand flax) strips with natural dyes

textiles.name: New Zealand Merino Wool
textiles.description: Ultrafine merino wool from high-country sheep stations, prized for its exceptional softness, warmth-to-weight ratio, and natural odor resistance, now a cornerstone of ethical fashion.
textiles.technique: Precision spinning and knitting of 15-20 micron merino fibers into performance and fashion fabrics

textiles.name: Harakeke (NZ Flax) Cloth
textiles.description: Traditional Maori textile woven from prepared native flax, producing a strong, flexible fabric used for cloaks, kits (baskets), and contemporary art pieces.
textiles.technique: Whatu (hand-twining) of softened harakeke fibers with aho (weft) and whenu (warp) strands

textiles.name: Possum-Merino Blend
textiles.description: A uniquely New Zealand innovation blending brushtail possum fur (an invasive pest) with merino wool, creating an exceptionally warm, lightweight, and sustainable luxury fiber.
textiles.technique: Blending 40% possum fur with 50% merino wool and 10% silk, spun into fine yarn

climate.fashionImplication: New Zealand's changeable, four-seasons-in-one-day weather drives a layering-centric wardrobe philosophy. The strong UV index despite mild temperatures creates demand for sun-protective fashion, while cool winds make merino knitwear a daily essential.

culturalInfluences: Maori tikanga (cultural protocols) and weaving traditions
British colonial heritage and rural practicality
Extreme outdoor and adventure sports culture
Geographic isolation fostering independent creative identity
Film and creative industries (Weta Workshop, Peter Jackson)
Environmental consciousness and clean-green national identity

emergingTrends: Regenerative merino wool and circular fashion
Maori design principles in contemporary fashion
